title.zh: 邊界之外： refugee 保護的後自由主義轉型
title.en: The Invisible Walls: How the Refugee System Is Being Reconfigured in a Postliberal World

summary.zh:
【總結】這項研究揭開全球 refugee 治理的隱形轉型：不是制度崩潰，而是「 containment（管制）」結構的重生。過去以《1951年 refugee 條約》為核心的保護體系，正透過邊界外移、法律模糊化與機構委託，重新建構為「後自由主義」治理模式。政府不再提供 asylum（庇護），而是用地理距離、程序拖延與第三方合作，既維持「合規」表象，又實質限制庇護權。這不僅是政策調整，更是國際秩序邏輯的根本轉變—— sovereignty（主權）與風險管理取代 multilateralism（多邊主義），成為新治理的基石。這項發現挑戰了「 refugee 機制瓦解」的常見論述，揭示了當代國際治理的深層邏輯。

【研究問題】文章探討「 refugee 治理如何在後自由主義國際秩序中轉型」。過去研究多聚焦於「 norm erosion（規範萎縮）」，卻忽略制度性轉型的系統性。作者指出：當前實踐並非單一政策失敗，而是「柔性 containment」的結構性轉型——政府透過邊界外移、法律替代與機構委託，既不放棄國際義務，又實質削弱庇護權。此問題關鍵在於：若不理解這套新機制，將無法應對當代 refugee 危機，更無法預見國際治理的未來。

【研究方法】作者運用「 institutional drift（制度漂移）」與「 legal substitution（法律替代）」兩大理論框架，分析全球 refugee 治理的變化。以「 EU-Turkey 2016 協議」、「 US MPP 政策」、「 UK-Rwanda 協定」等案例為核心，解構政府如何透過「地理外包」（如將 asylum 申請者送往土耳其或利比亞）、「程序延宕」（延長審查時間）與「機構委託」（讓 UNHCR 協助處理邊界事務）來調整義務。研究採用歷史比較與制度分析，將抽象理論轉化為具體操作機制，揭示「形式合規」如何掩蓋「實質剝奪」。

【結果】研究揭示：2016 年 EU-Turkey 協議使超過 20 萬名 asylum seekers 被遣返土耳其；美國「MPP 政策」讓 7 萬人被迫在墨西哥等待審查；2025 年美國最高法院同意將移民遣返至烏干達、盧旺達等「第三方國家」。這些案例顯示， refugee 治理已從「權利保障」轉向「風險管理」，保護權被重新定義為「政策裁量」。更關鍵的是，全球 149 個 UN 會員國仍簽署《 refugee 條約》，但實際執行中，「非強制性承諾」（如 Global Compact）已取代法律約束。這不僅是政策調整，更是國際治理邏輯的永久性轉變。
summary.en:
**Summary:** This article reveals a hidden transformation in global refugee governance: not a collapse of the system but the reconfiguration of a "containment architecture" under a postliberal order. The regime built on the 1951 Refugee Convention is shifting from providing asylum to managing displacement through border externalization, legal ambiguity, and institutional delegation. Governments maintain formal compliance while systematically restricting access to protection—turning refugee rights into policy discretion. This isn't merely policy adjustment but a fundamental shift in international governance logic, where sovereignty and risk management now override multilateralism and rights enforcement. The research challenges conventional narratives of "regime failure" by showing how the system adapts to new political realities.

**Problem:** The article addresses how refugee governance has evolved in a postliberal international order. Previous scholarship focused on norm erosion, but overlooked the systemic reconfiguration of the regime. The author argues that current practices—such as border externalization and legal substitution—are not isolated failures but a deliberate "flexible containment" structure. This matters because understanding this shift is critical to addressing refugee crises and predicting the future of global governance. Without recognizing this new logic, policymakers risk perpetuating ineffective responses to displacement.

**Approach:** The author uses "institutional drift" (gradual change within existing frameworks) and "legal substitution" (creating alternatives to the Refugee Convention) as analytical tools. Case studies include the 2016 EU-Turkey Statement (returning 200,000+ asylum seekers), the US MPP program (70,000+ processed in Mexico), and the UK-Rwanda agreement (deporting asylum seekers to distant third countries). The research traces how these mechanisms work together to maintain formal compliance while hollowing out substantive protection. By analyzing historical context and institutional dynamics, the author transforms abstract concepts into concrete operational practices.

**Results:** The study documents how 200,000+ asylum seekers were returned via the EU-Turkey deal; the US MPP program processed 70,000+ in Mexico; and the US Supreme Court recently allowed deportations to Uganda and Rwanda. These cases show that refugee protection is now framed as "risk management" rather than "rights enforcement." Crucially, 149 UN member states remain party to the Refugee Convention, but the Global Compact on Refugees institutionalizes "responsibility by proximity"—replacing binding commitments with voluntary pledges. This redefinition means protection is no longer a legal right but a policy choice, reshaping international governance for decades to come.

keyArticle.title: The Architecture of Containment: Refugee Protection in a Postliberal Order
